structured.summary: This paper proposes Automatic Prompt Engineer (APE), a method that uses large language models (LLMs) to automatically generate and select natural language instructions (prompts) to steer LLM behavior. APE formulates prompt engineering as a black-box optimization problem and demonstrates human-level or better performance on 24 NLP tasks, improving zero-shot and few-shot learning as well as truthfulness and informativeness in LLM outputs.

strengths.point: Novel formulation of prompt engineering as natural language program synthesis and black-box optimization.
strengths.evidence: Section 3 introduces framing instruction generation as program synthesis and black-box optimization guided by LLMs.

strengths.point: Strong empirical results showing APE outperforms prior model-generated prompts and matches or exceeds human prompt engineers on 21/24 tasks.
strengths.evidence: Section 4.1 presents zero-shot and few-shot results on 24 NLP tasks, with figures showing APE surpassing human and greedy baselines.

strengths.point: Demonstrated ability to steer LLMs toward desired behaviors such as truthfulness and informativeness.
strengths.evidence: Section 4.2 applies APE to TruthfulQA, showing improved truthfulness and a trade-off with informativeness compared to human prompts.

strengths.point: Comprehensive qualitative and quantitative analyses exploring proposal distributions, scoring functions, and iterative search.
strengths.evidence: Section 5 analyzes effects of model size, scoring metrics, and iterative Monte Carlo search on prompt quality.

weaknesses.point: Dependence on large, instruction-tuned LLMs for generating high-quality prompt candidates.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 5.1 shows smaller models produce lower quality proposals, requiring large models like InstructGPT (175B) for best results.

weaknesses.point: Limited exploration of generalization beyond the 24 NLP tasks and TruthfulQA dataset.
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments focus on a fixed set of tasks from Honovich et al. (2022) and TruthfulQA; broader applicability to other domains or modalities is not evaluated.

weaknesses.point: Iterative Monte Carlo search provides only marginal improvements over the simpler initial proposal method.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 5.2 and Figure 7 indicate iterative search yields diminishing returns and is not always necessary.

weaknesses.point: Potential overfitting to training demonstrations in instruction induction, limiting true zero-shot generalization.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.2 notes that optimizing instructions on a small training subset of TruthfulQA questions means results are not strictly zero-shot.

weaknesses.point: Some tasks show performance degradation when combining APE instructions with in-context examples.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.1 discusses adversarial cases like Rhymes and Second Letters where adding context harms accuracy.

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on computational cost and efficiency of the APE search process.
weaknesses.evidence: While Section 3.2 mentions adaptive filtering to reduce computation, the overall resource requirements for large-scale prompt search are not detailed.

weaknesses.point: Lack of human evaluation for truthfulness and informativeness metrics in TruthfulQA experiments.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.2 relies on automated GPT-judge metrics without human validation of generated answers.

questions.question: How sensitive is APE to the choice and number of demonstrations used for initial prompt generation?
questions.motivation: Section 4.1 notes variance in instruction quality depending on demonstration subsets; understanding this sensitivity is important for practical deployment.

questions.question: Can APE be extended or adapted to multimodal models or tasks beyond NLP, such as image generation?
questions.motivation: The paper mentions potential applicability to other models with natural language interfaces but does not empirically explore this.

questions.question: What are the computational costs and latency associated with running APE in practice, especially for large models?
questions.motivation: Understanding resource requirements is critical for assessing feasibility and scalability of the approach.

questions.question: Have the authors considered combining APE with human-in-the-loop prompt refinement to leverage complementary strengths?
questions.motivation: Human expertise might help guide or validate the search, potentially improving efficiency and robustness.

critique_points.point_001: Dependence on large, instruction-tuned LLMs for generating high-quality prompt candidates. (Section 5.1 shows smaller models produce lower quality proposals, requiring large models like InstructGPT (175B) for best results.)
critique_points.point_002: Limited exploration of generalization beyond the 24 NLP tasks and TruthfulQA dataset. (Experiments focus on a fixed set of tasks from Honovich et al. (2022) and TruthfulQA; broader applicability to other domains or modalities is not evaluated.)
critique_points.point_003: Iterative Monte Carlo search provides only marginal improvements over the simpler initial proposal method. (Section 5.2 and Figure 7 indicate iterative search yields diminishing returns and is not always necessary.)
critique_points.point_004: Potential overfitting to training demonstrations in instruction induction, limiting true zero-shot generalization. (Section 4.2 notes that optimizing instructions on a small training subset of TruthfulQA questions means results are not strictly zero-shot.)
critique_points.point_005: Some tasks show performance degradation when combining APE instructions with in-context examples. (Section 4.1 discusses adversarial cases like Rhymes and Second Letters where adding context harms accuracy.)
critique_points.point_006: Limited discussion on computational cost and efficiency of the APE search process. (While Section 3.2 mentions adaptive filtering to reduce computation, the overall resource requirements for large-scale prompt search are not detailed.)
critique_points.point_007: Lack of human evaluation for truthfulness and informativeness metrics in TruthfulQA experiments. (Section 4.2 relies on automated GPT-judge metrics without human validation of generated answers.)
critique_points.point_008: How sensitive is APE to the choice and number of demonstrations used for initial prompt generation? (Section 4.1 notes variance in instruction quality depending on demonstration subsets; understanding this sensitivity is important for practical deployment.)
critique_points.point_009: Can APE be extended or adapted to multimodal models or tasks beyond NLP, such as image generation? (The paper mentions potential applicability to other models with natural language interfaces but does not empirically explore this.)
critique_points.point_010: What are the computational costs and latency associated with running APE in practice, especially for large models? (Understanding resource requirements is critical for assessing feasibility and scalability of the approach.)
critique_points.point_011: Have the authors considered combining APE with human-in-the-loop prompt refinement to leverage complementary strengths? (Human expertise might help guide or validate the search, potentially improving efficiency and robustness.)
